risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "participating ASUs will be stratified and randomised... Stratification details will be provided in a de-identified form to an independent statistician located offshore and not otherwise involved in the study for randomisation within strata, this will be generated using random number generating software." Comment: The paper explicitly describes stratified randomisation performed by an independent statistician using random number generating software, which is a proper random allocation method.

Quote: "Stratification details will be provided in a de-identified form to an independent statistician located offshore and not otherwise involved in the study for randomisation within strata... Allocation will be based on clusters (ASUs) rather than individuals, and the sequence will be concealed until the intervention is assigned. Thus, generation of the allocation sequence and assigning of ASUs to either intervention or control group will be undertaken by the offshore independent statistician." Comment: This explicitly states that the allocation sequence was concealed until assignment and that an independent offshore statistician handled randomisation, which is sufficient to prevent foreknowledge by recruiters.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients will be blinded to group allocation." and "Both the medical director and NUM of all consenting ASUs will be aware that our study is examining the effect of an intervention... control ASUs may be able to deduce their group allocation because no workshops are being organised." Comment: This cluster trial delivered a service-level behavioural intervention to staff, so participants/personnel delivering care could not be adequately blinded and likely knew allocation. Because the outcomes include care processes (e.g., temperature, glucose, swallowing screening) and patient-dependent functional outcomes, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence performance and thus the outcomes overall.

Quote: "All CATIs will be undertaken by research interviewers blind to the study design and also to ASU group allocation" and "Blinded outcome assessment will be undertaken for all analyses of primary and secondary outcome measures." Comment: The key patient outcomes (90-day mRS, Barthel Index, SF-36) were assessed by CATI interviewers explicitly blinded to allocation, so patient self-report outcome measurement was not likely influenced by knowledge of group assignment. In addition, chart-audit outcomes were collected by independent research assistants blind to group allocation, supporting low risk for detection bias across the reported outcomes.

Quote: "Our 90-day follow-up will comprise a computer-assisted telephone interview (CATI)..." and "Intention-to-treat analysis will applied" and "For missing data, patient clinical data will be obtained from the TASC database." Comment: The protocol indicates planned 90-day follow-up with blinded interviewers, ITT analysis, and a strategy to obtain missing clinical data from the TASC database, which suggests attrition/missingness was anticipated and handled in a way unlikely to bias results. The paper does not report problematic or imbalanced dropout in the methods text provided.
